Question: In one of its Spring catalogs, L.L. Bean® advertised footwear on 29 of its 192 catalog pages. Suppose we randomly survey 20 pages. We are interested in the number of pages that advertise footwear. Each page may be picked at most once. a) In words, define the random variable X . b) List the values that X may take on. c) How many pages do you expect to advertise footwear on them? d) Calculate the standard deviation.
Answer: a) X = the number of pages that advertise footwear b) 0, 1, 2, 3, ..., 20 c) 3.03 d) 1.5197

 population size N = 192 pages  
 number of “successes’’ in the population K = 29 footwear pages  
 sample size n = 20 pages  

a)  Random variable  
   X = “the number of pages, out of the 20 pages sampled, that advertise footwear.’’

b)  Possible values of X  
   The smallest number of footwear pages we could see is 0; the largest is the smaller of 20 and 29, i.e. 20.  
   Therefore     X = 0, 1, 2, … , 20.

c)  Expected number of sampled pages that advertise footwear  
   For a hyper-geometric r.v.  E[X] = n (K / N):

        E[X] = 20 × (29 / 192) = 20 × 0.1510417 ≈ 3.02 pages.

d)  Standard deviation  
   Variance of a hyper-geometric r.v.:

        Var(X) = n (K / N) (1 − K / N) · (N − n) / (N − 1)

        = 20 (29/192)(163/192) · (172/191)
        ≈ 2.31

   Standard deviation:

        σ = √Var(X) ≈ √2.31 ≈ 1.52 pages.

Summary  
 E[X] ≈ 3.02 pages  
 σ ≈ 1.52 pages.

If two of the dice show the number or object bet (and the third die does not show it), the player gets back their $1 bet, plus $2 profit. Answer: 1) X = the number of matches 2) 0, 1, 2, 3 3) In dollars: -1, 1, 2, 3 4) $$\frac{1}{2}$$ 5) The answer is -0.0787. You lose about eight cents, on average, per game. 6) The house has the advantage.

Question: The chance of having an extra fortune in a fortune cookie is about 3%. Given a bag of 144 fortune cookies, we are interested in the number of cookies with an extra fortune. Two distributions may be used to solve this problem, but only use one distribution to solve the problem. a) In words, define the random variable X . b) List the values that X may take on. c) How many cookies do we expect to have an extra fortune? d) Find the probability that none of the cookies have an extra fortune. e) Find the probability that more than three have an extra fortune. f) As n increases, what happens involving the probabilities using the two distributions? Answer: a) X = the number of fortune cookies that have an extra fortune b) 0, 1, 2, 3,... 144 c) 4.32 d) 0.0124 or 0.0133 e) 0.6300 or 0.6264 f) As n gets larger, the probabilities get closer together.
o3 Answer:
We shall model the situation with a Poisson distribution.  
(The exact model is Binomial(n = 144, p = 0.03) but, because n is large and p is small, the Poisson(λ = n p) approximation is customary and much simpler.)

a) Random-variable definition  
X = the number of the 144 cookies that contain an extra fortune.

b) Possible values of X  
X can be any non-negative integer; in practice it can be 0, 1, 2, …, 144 (under the Poisson model the upper bound is not needed, but probabilities above 144 are essentially 0).

c) Expected (mean) number with an extra fortune  
λ = n p = 144 (0.03) = 4.32  
E[X] = λ = 4.32 cookies.  (On average a little more than four cookies.)

d) Probability that none of the cookies has an extra fortune  
P(X = 0) = e^−λ = e^−4.32 ≈ 0.0133

e) Probability that more than three cookies have an extra fortune  
P(X > 3) = 1 – P(X ≤ 3)

Compute the first four Poisson probabilities:

P0 = e^−4.32 = 0.013318  
P1 = e^−4.32 (4.32)^1 / 1! = 0.057973  
P2 = e^−4.32 (4.32)^2 / 2! = 0.124288  
P3 = e^−4.32 (4.32)^3 / 3! = 0.179156  

P(X ≤ 3) = P0 + P1 + P2 + P3 ≈ 0.3747  
P(X > 3) = 1 – 0.3747 ≈ 0.6253

So there is about a 62.5 % chance that more than three cookies contain an extra fortune.

f) Relationship of the two distributions as n grows  
If n increases while p stays small so that λ = n p remains moderate, the Binomial(n, p) distribution converges to the Poisson(λ) distribution. Consequently, the probabilities computed from the Poisson formula get closer and closer to the exact binomial probabilities, making the Poisson a very accurate—and much easier—approximation for large n.
